lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — The frontier
Let me close by looking forward.
There are several directions where cancer research is heading that I think will be reshaping the field over the next decade or two.
Personalized cancer vaccines.
I want you to appreciate how strange and beautiful this idea is.
Every cancer carries its own unique set of mutations — drivers and passengers both.
Some of those mutations create slightly weird protein fragments — neoantigens — that the immune system has never seen before and could, in principle, learn to attack as foreign.

The idea of a personalized cancer vaccine is: sequence the patient's tumor, identify the neoantigens unique to that patient's cancer, design an mRNA vaccine encoding those neoantigens (the same mRNA technology that drove COVID vaccines), and give the patient a vaccine custom-built to teach their immune system to attack their specific cancer.
This was the realm of science fiction five years ago.
As of now, it's in late-stage trials, especially in combination with checkpoint inhibitors.
Early results in melanoma look genuinely promising.
Companies like BioNTech and Moderna, riding their COVID-era manufacturing capability, can now produce a personalized vaccine for an individual patient in a few weeks.
We may be heading toward a world where, on the day you're diagnosed with cancer, a sample of your tumor goes to a facility that ships you back a vaccine tailored to it.
Base editing and prime editing.
CRISPR you've probably heard of — the gene-editing tool that won a Nobel Prize in 2020.
Newer tools called base editors and prime editors are refinements that let you change individual DNA letters with much less collateral damage.
In cancer, the immediate application is engineering better cell therapies — making CAR-T cells more durable, more potent, less prone to exhaustion.
You could imagine, over time, even using these tools therapeutically to correct cancer-driving mutations in a patient's own cells.
Organoids.
This is one of my favorite developments.

We can now take a small biopsy of a patient's tumor, grow it as a three-dimensional mini-tumor in a dish — an organoid that recapitulates much of the original tumor's biology — and then test a panel of drugs against that organoid to see which one actually kills the patient's specific cancer best.
It's a personalized drug-sensitivity test, done outside the patient.

It's not yet routinely used in clinical decision-making, but it's getting there, and for cancers where we have multiple plausible drug options, organoid screening could become an extremely valuable tiebreaker.
Drugging the undruggable.
Remember how we said most oncogenes are stuck gas pedals that we can drug, but tumor suppressors are missing brakes that we can't?
New technologies are starting to chip at that asymmetry.
PROTACs — proteolysis targeting chimeras — are bifunctional molecules that grab a target protein and drag it over to the cell's protein-degradation machinery, marking it for destruction.
They can take out proteins that have no good drug pocket of their own.
Molecular glues stick two proteins together that don't normally interact, often again leading to one of them being destroyed.
These tools are starting to access target proteins that small molecules never could.
Genome-scale synthetic lethality screens.
Using CRISPR libraries, researchers can systematically knock out every gene in the genome, one at a time, in a cancer cell line and ask: which gene knockouts kill this specific cancer?

The hope is to find new synthetic lethal pairs — like the BRCA-PARP one — so that for any given cancer driver mutation, we can find a second target whose loss is specifically lethal in that genetic background.
Microbiome and immunotherapy.
This is a strange one.
Patients with certain gut microbiome compositions respond much better to checkpoint inhibitors than patients with other compositions.
There are clinical trials underway of fecal microbiota transplants given alongside immunotherapy, trying to shift the gut microbiome of non-responders into a responder profile.
The idea that whether a melanoma drug works might depend on your gut bacteria would have been laughed out of the room twenty years ago.
Now it's mainstream research.
Let me close with a thought.
I started this lecture with the framing that cancer is a cell that has forgotten the rules of cooperation.
Two hours later, you've now seen what it takes to actually disrupt that defection.

You need to understand what gas pedals are stuck, what brakes are broken, how the assembly line has been corrupted, why the suicide program failed, how the cell learned to travel and to recruit a neighborhood, how the immune system was made to look away.
And for each of those failures, the field has developed — and is still developing — ways to push back.
A century ago, a cancer diagnosis was, mostly, a death sentence.
Forty years ago, it was a death sentence with chemo as a coin flip.
Today, for many cancers, it's a complicated chronic condition, sometimes a curable disease, and for a growing list of patients, something they can expect to live many years with or beyond.
The trajectory is real.
Cancer is becoming, slowly, surgically, drug by drug and idea by idea, a problem we are starting to actually solve rather than just suffer through.
We're not done.
We may never be done — cancer is, after all, the body's own cells finding new ways to defect, and evolution has many moves.
But the toolbox we've assembled, the toolbox you've just spent two hours getting a tour of, is genuinely something to be proud of as a species.
You're walking out of this room understanding cancer better than the smartest oncologist in the world did in 1980.
That's the field.
That's the pace.
And many of you, if you go into this area, will be part of the next chapter.
Thanks, everyone.
Take care of yourselves.
Good work today.
